chunks.text: Interface Mechanisms and System Visualization in Component-Based Models Interface Mechanisms and System Visualization in Component-Based Models Researchers: Paul Whitaker Advisor: Edward A. Lee This work involves system visualization and interface de

ork involves system visualization and interface design. In a system such as Ptolemy II, it can be very desirable for components in the model to be capable of providing information about themselves or accepting input from the user. A block diagram

or accepting input from the user. A block diagram view, using icons, is often an intuitive way to visualize a system. Within Vergil , the Ptolemy II user interface, components of the model are displayed as icons. Dynamic icons are a natural way

played as icons. Dynamic icons are a natural way of expressing information regarding the characteristics and status of particular components. Icons are also a natural receptacle for user input. The Java Foundation Classes provide a rich collec

The Java Foundation Classes provide a rich collection of user-interface components. This work requires using these components within Vergil (and Diva , the graphics framework on which it is based). It is possible that configurability options cou

. It is possible that configurability options could be specified using the MoML XML schema. Like Vergil itself, this work is intended to be sufficiently general to be applicable to other component-based design tools. Last updated 11/02/00
